evidence: goseg/web/_app/immutable/chunks/websocket.84e20bf3.js centralizes transport lifecycle (`p=new WebSocket(t)`), auth/token storage (`localStorage.setItem`), inbound state routing, and a very large command surface (`j`, `q`, `V`, `Y`, `F`, `H`, `K`, `ot`, `lt`, `kt`, etc.) in one module.

Multiple entry modules directly depend on that same compiled websocket hub (e.g., imports from `../chunks/websocket.84e20bf3.js` in `entry/apps-page.svelte.85e3c543.js`, `entry/boot-existing-page.svelte.509e2e47.js`, and `entry/captive-page.svelte.525c6d92.js`), increasing blast radius.

goseg/web/_app/immutable/chunks/urbit.f551ce98.js creates a module-level singleton (`const f=new v("")`) and installs browser lifecycle behavior in constructor (`window.addEventListener("beforeunload", this.delete)`), creating import-time side effects and hidden lifecycle coupling.

The handwritten runtime path (`ui/src/lib/runtime/transport/websocket-client.js`, `ui/src/lib/stores/logsocket.js`, `ui/src/lib/runtime/commands/system-commands.js`, `ui/src/lib/runtime/commands/ship-commands.js`) duplicates command/transport concerns that are also embedded in compiled `chunks/websocket.84e20bf3.js`, indicating parallel architectural paths.
holistic_context.structure shows very large generated directories: goseg/web/_app/immutable/chunks/ has 49 files and goseg/web/_app/immutable/entry/ has 31 files, both zoned generated.

Assigned generated files are hash-addressed and ownership-obscuring (for example chunks/0.f72efb1a.js, 1.0d44cf41.js, 10.35bd59a0.js), while functional source for behavior exists elsewhere (ui/src/lib/runtime/commands/*.js), splitting runtime ownership across build outputs and source trees.

ui/src/lib/runtime/commands/ is a flat 17-file directory (15 production + 2 tests) containing heterogeneous domains (auth, setup, urbit, network, support, system) plus shared infrastructure (context.js).
context.test.js is colocated with all command implementations in the same high-count folder, increasing mixed-concern navigation inside an already overloaded directory.

The documented realtime contract declares `send` as Promise-returning and `connect(options)` as void in `ui/src/lib/runtime/contracts/realtime-client.js`, but `ui/src/lib/runtime/transport/websocket-client.js` implements synchronous `send` result objects and `connect()` returning a WebSocket session.

Command modules are largely pass-through wrappers around transport calls (`createShipCommands`, `createSystemCommands`, `createAuthCommands`) with repeated hand-written payload assembly and ad-hoc parameter renaming (`pwd`->`password`, `val`->`value`).

`ui/src/lib/stores/logsocket.js` combines socket lifecycle management, auth token retrieval, message parsing, and Svelte store mutation in one module, and `connect()` triggers async `requestLogs()` in `onOpen` without awaiting or surfacing failures.

findings.summary: A single websocket chunk acts as a cross-cutting god module, coupling transport, auth persistence, state dispatch, and dozens of domain commands.
evidence: `chunks/websocket.84e20bf3.js` contains transport setup (`p=new WebSocket(t)`), retry logic (`setTimeout(()=>M(t),1e3)`), token persistence (`localStorage.setItem`), event routing (`D=t=>...`), and a long list of command wrappers (`j`, `q`, `V`, `Y`, `K`, `ot`, `lt`, `kt`, etc.) in one file.
Entry bundles import this module directly, making page-level behavior depend on a broad shared hub rather than narrower capability interfaces.
Because commands and socket lifecycle are co-located, changing one domain action shape forces edits in the same high-fanout module that handles unrelated domains.
findings.suggestion: Split the websocket chunk responsibilities behind stable boundaries: isolate transport/session lifecycle, isolate auth token persistence, and expose domain command adapters from per-domain modules. Keep page entries importing only the minimal adapter they need.

findings.summary: Module evaluation in the Urbit realtime chunk performs hidden lifecycle setup through singleton construction and global event listener registration.
evidence: `urbit.f551ce98.js` constructs `const f=new v("")` at module scope, so stateful runtime object creation happens on import.
Class `v` constructor conditionally registers `window.addEventListener("beforeunload", this.delete)`, coupling module import order to browser lifecycle behavior.
The same file mutates singleton callbacks (`f.onOpen=...`, `f.onRetry=...`, `f.onError=...`) later via exported functions, creating implicit global state transitions across consumers.
findings.suggestion: Remove module-scope singleton instantiation and replace with an explicit factory/init phase managed by an orchestrator module; register/unregister browser lifecycle hooks during connect/disconnect, not at import time.

findings.summary: Two parallel realtime/command architectures coexist (compiled websocket hub vs runtime command+transport modules), creating duplicated boundaries and drift risk.
evidence: Compiled websocket chunk defines many command payload constructors (`{type:"system",action:"groundseg",command:"restart"}`, `{type:"system",action:"wifi-connect",...}`, `{type:"pier_upload",...}`, `{type:"new_ship",...}`), while runtime commands modules define overlapping command intents via `sendSystemCommand` and `sendActionCommand`.

`ui/src/lib/stores/logsocket.js` introduces a separate runtime websocket client and token-loading flow (`createWebSocketClient`, `loadSession`) instead of sharing one realtime service boundary with the compiled path.
This duplicate command/transport surface means policy changes (validation, retries, auth shape) can diverge between stacks.
findings.suggestion: Converge to a single runtime command and realtime service layer, then make generated/entry code consume that layer through stable adapters. Eliminate duplicated command-shape ownership from transport modules.

findings.summary: The declared realtime contract is not an active boundary; implementation and consumers bypass or diverge from it.
evidence: Contract declares `send` returns `Promise<any>` and `connect(options: { ship?, onEvent?, onError? })`.
`createWebSocketClient` implements `connect()` with no options parameter and `send(data)` returning synchronous status objects (`{ok:false,...}` / `{ok:true}`), not a Promise.
`logsocket.js` consumes concrete implementation details (`isOpen`, `getReadyState`, `send` return shape) rather than a contract-checked interface.
findings.suggestion: Make `realtime-client` a real interface boundary: align method signatures/return types, add adapter tests for contract conformance, and type/check consumer code against the contract rather than concrete helper methods.

findings.summary: Committed hashed bundle files create a parallel package tree that obscures ownership boundaries.
evidence: Assigned chunk files are hash-named wrappers/minified modules with no domain semantics, so directory navigation does not reflect feature ownership.

Entry files import hashed chunk modules (for example (home)-page imports multiple ../chunks/*.js artifacts), reinforcing dependency mapping at artifact level instead of source-level package boundaries.
structure profile reports 80 generated files overall, with the two immutable directories dominating file count, indicating package surface is weighted toward build artifacts.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) create explicit artifact boundary folders such as ui/build/client/ and goseg/web/static/client/ (or a single canonical artifact root), (2) move immutable output generation target to that boundary and stop treating hashed artifacts as source-owned package content, (3) keep feature ownership in source folders only (ui/src/routes, ui/src/lib/*) and retain only manifest + deployment-required outputs in goseg, (4) update import/build wiring via bundler output config and backend static-path config, then run `npm --prefix ui run build`, `rg "_app/immutable" -n`, and project test/build commands to validate no runtime path regressions.

findings.summary: Runtime commands are kept in an overloaded flat directory mixing multiple domains and support layers.
evidence: commands directory profile has 17 files (>10 threshold) with mixed concerns and layers (domain command modules, shared command context, and tests).
auth-commands.js is domain behavior but depends on generic transport helpers from context.js, yet both live at same package depth without sub-boundaries.
context.test.js sits in the same flat directory as production command modules, increasing folder entropy during feature/domain navigation.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) introduce subfolders `ui/src/lib/runtime/commands/core/` (context + shared senders), `ui/src/lib/runtime/commands/domains/` (auth/system/setup/urbit/etc), and `ui/src/lib/runtime/commands/__tests__/` or mirrored test folders, (2) move core first (`context.js`, `context.test.js`) and add temporary barrel exports, (3) move domain modules in small batches and update imports after each batch with `rg "from './context|from '../commands" ui/src/lib/runtime -n`, (4) remove temporary barrels once callers are migrated, then validate with `npm --prefix ui test` and `npm --prefix ui run build`.

findings.summary: Hashed runtime modules are tightly coupled without an enforceable atomic compatibility contract.
evidence: README mandates generated runtime ownership but does not define a lockstep publish rule for hashed asset sets.
`apps-page.svelte.85e3c543.js` imports `../chunks/websocket.84e20bf3.js`; this creates hard hash-level coupling between entry and chunk artifacts.
Any partial refresh of committed generated files can leave import references stale without a documented compatibility check.
findings.suggestion: Add an explicit build-compatibility contract: generate a manifest (entry->chunk hash map + source commit + build tool versions), require CI to run `cd ui && npm ci && npm run build`, compare regenerated `goseg/web/_app/immutable/` against committed artifacts, and fail on partial/hash-inconsistent updates; document this atomic publish rule in README.

findings.summary: Committed generated runtime lacks traceability metadata to its source boundary.
evidence: README states source-of-truth is `ui/src/`, but reviewed generated files are minified bundles with hashed names and no embedded origin/provenance markers.
`urbit.f551ce98.js` and `boot-existing-page.svelte.509e2e47.js` expose runtime behavior but do not indicate source module lineage or build identity in-file.
This weakens architecture-governance review because boundary drift cannot be audited from committed runtime artifacts alone.
findings.suggestion: Emit and commit machine-readable provenance alongside immutable output (for example `goseg/web/_app/immutable/manifest.json` with source path mapping, source commit SHA, and build timestamp/toolchain), then link that artifact from README and enforce its refresh in CI.

findings.summary: Generated immutable artifacts are organized as primary package structure instead of a bounded distribution package.
evidence: Structure profile shows large generated directories (`entry/` 31 files, `chunks/` 49 files), indicating flat artifact-heavy layout beyond cohesive package boundaries.
Reviewed files mix feature semantics (apps/captive/boot) with bundler-oriented placement (`entry` vs `chunks`), which is optimized for runtime loading, not repository ownership.
README warns against hand-editing these files, confirming they are distribution outputs, yet they remain exposed under a path that behaves like ordinary package structure.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) create `goseg/web/generated/<build-id>/` as the sole committed distribution package with `manifest.json`; (2) move `entry/*` first, then `chunks/*`, preserving relative references via manifest-driven path rewrite; (3) update serving/config references to the new generated root; (4) run `cd ui && npm ci && npm run build` then validate with `rg -n "_app/immutable|generated/<build-id>" README.md goseg` and a startup smoke test to confirm imports resolve.

findings.summary: Realtime interface contract and websocket implementation diverge, making abstractions unreliable.
evidence: Contract typedef says `send(payload) => Promise<any>` and `connect(options) => void`.
Implementation exposes `connect()` with no options and returns a raw session object; `send()` returns sync status objects like `{ ok: false, reason: 'not_open' }`.
Consumer code in `logsocket.js` treats `send()` as sync result and depends on extra methods (`isOpen`, `getReadyState`) not represented in the contract.
findings.suggestion: Define one authoritative runtime interface and align all producers/consumers: either update the contract to the actual sync status API (including readiness helpers) or adapt `websocket-client` to the documented async shape; add contract-level tests asserting function signatures and result semantics.

findings.summary: Command modules repeat boilerplate wrapper patterns instead of using a coherent command schema.
evidence: Most exported command functions only forward constants plus lightly reshaped payloads into `sendActionCommand`/`sendTypeCommand`.
Parameter naming and payload shaping is inconsistent across wrappers (`pwd`, `val`, `old`) and each function hand-encodes type/action strings.
Validation policy is uneven: `bootShip` uses `sendValidatedCommand`, while other wrappers with required intent fields bypass it entirely.
findings.suggestion: Replace per-function boilerplate with a shared command descriptor table (type/action/required fields/payload mapper), then generate handlers from descriptors so naming, validation, and transport behavior are centralized and consistent.

findings.summary: Log socket store module mixes unrelated responsibilities and hides failure paths.
evidence: `logsocket.js` owns session registry (`sessionsByType`), websocket event wiring, token loading (`loadSession()`), request serialization, parse handling, and UI state mutation (`logs.update`) in one file.
`connect()` returns `{ ok: true }` immediately after `socketClient.connect()` regardless of actual open/error outcomes.
`onOpen` calls async `requestLogs(logType)` without handling the returned error result, so bootstrapping failures are silent to callers.
findings.suggestion: Split the module into a transport/session service and a pure store reducer layer; make connect/request lifecycle explicit by returning a promise or evented status that includes initial subscription success/failure.
